title: Vanguard S&P 500 ETF
headline: The lowest-friction way to own America's 500 biggest businesses.
summary: VOO holds every stock in the S&P 500 in proportion to its market cap. When the index moves, VOO moves. It's the most-cited 'just buy the index' product for a reason — minimal fees, maximum diversification across the largest US companies.
mechanics: Vanguard buys and rebalances the underlying 500 stocks to mirror the index. You own a tiny slice of each. Distributions arrive quarterly. The structure is highly tax-efficient inside US accounts and reasonably efficient inside Canadian RRSPs.
whoItsFor: Long-horizon investors who want broad US equity exposure without picking winners. The default building block for most portfolios.

title: Invesco QQQ Trust
headline: The closest thing to a single-ticker bet on US large-cap tech and innovation.
summary: QQQ tracks the 100 largest non-financial companies on the Nasdaq. In practice that means Apple, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Amazon, Meta, and the rest of the megacap tech complex — plus a tail of biotech and consumer names.
mechanics: You own a slice of each of the 100 underlying companies, weighted by market cap with some caps to prevent any single name from dominating. Higher concentration than VOO, higher beta, higher volatility.
whoItsFor: Investors who already have core diversified exposure and want to lean into US technology and innovation. Not a starter holding.

title: Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF
headline: Every publicly traded US stock — large, mid, and small — in one ticker.
summary: VTI holds roughly 3,700 US companies, weighted by market cap. It's VOO plus the long tail of mid- and small-caps. For most investors the difference vs VOO is rounding error, but you get a touch more diversification.
mechanics: Same index-replication approach as VOO. Distributions paid quarterly. Tax-efficient ETF structure.
whoItsFor: Investors who want true 'own everything' US exposure and are slightly more comfortable with small-cap volatility.

title: iShares Semiconductor ETF
headline: A concentrated, high-beta bet on the entire semiconductor value chain.
summary: SOXX holds about 30 semiconductor companies — design (NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom), manufacturing (TSMC ADR), and equipment (ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research). When AI capex is hot, this ETF screams; when it cools, expect 30-40% drawdowns.
mechanics: Modified market-cap weighting with a single-stock cap of 8% to limit concentration. Rebalanced quarterly. Roughly 70% US-domiciled, 30% foreign issuers via ADRs.
whoItsFor: Investors with conviction on the AI/compute thesis and tolerance for sharp drawdowns. Not a core holding.

title: Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund
headline: Concentrated US large-cap tech — the megacap winners with no biotech distractions.
summary: XLK holds only the technology-classified members of the S&P 500. That means Apple, Microsoft, NVIDIA, Broadcom, Cisco, Salesforce, etc. — without consumer internet names like Amazon and Meta (which sit in different sectors).
mechanics: Holds about 65 stocks, market-cap weighted with single-stock caps. Rebalanced quarterly. Tax-efficient SPDR structure.
whoItsFor: Investors who want pure-play US tech without paying QQQ's ~0.20% MER. Cheaper, more concentrated, marginally different basket.

title: Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF
headline: A quality-screened dividend basket that has quietly outperformed most income strategies.
summary: SCHD doesn't chase the highest yields — it screens for companies with at least 10 years of dividends, strong cash flow, and reasonable payout ratios. The result is a ~3.5% yielding basket that has actually grown its distribution faster than inflation for a decade.
mechanics: Annual rebalance based on the index methodology. Concentrated in financials, healthcare, consumer staples, and industrials. Lower tech weight than the broad market — by design.
whoItsFor: Income-focused investors who want a yield with growth, not a yield with risk. Pairs nicely with QQQ or VOO as a quality counterweight.

title: Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF
headline: A broad, cheap, above-average-yield basket of large-cap US stocks.
summary: VYM holds about 450 US stocks with above-average dividend yields. It's more diversified than SCHD but with looser quality screens — you get more sectors but also some dividend traps.
mechanics: Market-cap weighted within the universe of high-yield stocks. Rebalanced annually. Distributions paid quarterly.
whoItsFor: Investors who want broad income exposure with a lower MER than most active dividend funds.

title: Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF
tracksIndexName: Dow Jones US Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market
headline: Cheap, concentrated US large-cap growth — a quieter alternative to QQQ.
summary: SCHG holds about 230 of the largest US growth stocks. Heavy tech weighting, but with consumer discretionary and communications names included. Lower MER than QQQ and a slightly different basket.
mechanics: Index-replication, market-cap weighted. Reconstituted annually. Distributions paid quarterly.
whoItsFor: Investors who want US growth exposure at a rock-bottom fee and don't need the strict Nasdaq-100 methodology of QQQ.

title: Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (CAD)
headline: The S&P 500 in Canadian dollars — the easiest US equity holding for a TFSA or non-registered account.
summary: VFV gives Canadian investors S&P 500 exposure in CAD without converting to USD. Same underlying basket as VOO, just listed on the TSX and priced in loonies. Currency unhedged — you ride the USD/CAD swing.
mechanics: VFV holds VOO under the hood. The fund handles the USD-to-CAD price conversion for you. Distributions are paid in CAD. Withholding tax on US dividends still applies; hold inside an RRSP to avoid it.
whoItsFor: Canadians who want US large-cap equity exposure inside a TFSA or taxable account without the friction of Norbert's gambit or USD conversion.

title: BMO S&P 500 Index ETF
headline: BMO's CAD-listed S&P 500 — the head-to-head competitor to VFV.
summary: ZSP is a near-mirror of VFV: same index, same currency-unhedged exposure, similar MER. The choice between them mostly comes down to which broker offers commission-free purchases and personal preference.
mechanics: Holds the underlying 500 US stocks (or a sampling) directly. Distributions paid quarterly in CAD. Currency unhedged.
whoItsFor: Canadians who want low-cost S&P 500 exposure and prefer the BMO ecosystem.

title: iShares NASDAQ 100 Index ETF (CAD-Hedged)
headline: Nasdaq-100 in CAD with currency hedging — pure US tech beta without the FX risk.
summary: XQQ tracks the Nasdaq-100 but hedges the US dollar exposure back to CAD. When USD weakens, XQQ outperforms VFV/ZSP and the unhedged equivalents. When USD strengthens, the opposite.
mechanics: Currency forwards hedge USD exposure on a monthly basis. Holdings mirror QQQ. Some tracking error from hedging cost.
whoItsFor: Canadian investors with strong tech conviction who don't want to take a directional view on USD/CAD. Best for CAD-based goals.

title: iShares Core Equity ETF Portfolio
headline: A one-ticket all-equity portfolio: US, Canada, international developed, and emerging markets.
summary: XEQT is BlackRock's answer to 'I want one ETF and to never think about it again.' It holds four other iShares index funds in roughly 45% US, 25% Canada, 25% international developed, 5% emerging markets — and rebalances itself.
mechanics: BlackRock manages the underlying weightings for you. You buy one ticker and instantly own thousands of stocks across every major equity market. Distributions paid quarterly in CAD.
whoItsFor: New investors and anyone who wants a 'set it and forget it' all-equity core. The natural alternative to VEQT, with similar holdings and a marginally lower MER.

title: Vanguard All-Equity ETF Portfolio
headline: Vanguard's one-ticket global equity portfolio — XEQT's biggest rival.
summary: VEQT is structurally identical to XEQT: roughly 40% US, 30% Canada, 25% developed international, and 5% emerging markets. Both are excellent. The only meaningful differences are MER (slightly higher than XEQT's lately) and the slight tilt toward Canada.
mechanics: Holds Vanguard sub-funds and rebalances automatically. Distributions paid quarterly in CAD.
whoItsFor: Set-and-forget Canadian investors who prefer Vanguard's brand and a slightly more home-biased portfolio.

title: iShares Core S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF
headline: The default Canada-equity exposure — every meaningful TSX stock in one cheap ticker.
summary: XIC tracks the S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index, which holds about 235 Canadian stocks with a 10% cap on any single name. Heavy in financials and energy — the structural reality of the Canadian market.
mechanics: Index replication. Distributions paid quarterly in CAD. Tax-efficient ETF structure.
whoItsFor: Anyone who wants Canadian equity exposure without picking individual banks and pipelines. The 25% Canada slice of most XEQT/VEQT portfolios is essentially this.

title: Vanguard FTSE Canada All Cap Index ETF
headline: Broad Canadian equity exposure including small- and micro-caps.
summary: VCN is Vanguard's Canadian equity fund. Slightly broader than XIC because it includes more small-cap names. Same heavy financials/energy bias as the rest of the TSX.
mechanics: Index replication. Distributions paid quarterly in CAD.
whoItsFor: Investors who want home-market equity exposure and the small-cap tail.

title: BMO Equal Weight Banks Index ETF
tracksIndexName: Solactive Equal Weight Canada Banks Index
headline: Equal-weight exposure to the Big Six Canadian banks — a dividend-growth cornerstone.
summary: ZEB holds RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, CIBC, and National Bank in roughly equal proportions. The result is a cleaner banks-only basket than what you'd get from XIC or VCN, where banks are still ~25-30% of the portfolio.
mechanics: Equal-weighted, rebalanced semi-annually. Pays monthly distributions — popular with retirees.
whoItsFor: Income investors who want concentrated Big Six exposure and consider Canadian banks oligopoly-grade compounders.

title: Vanguard Canadian Aggregate Bond Index ETF
tracksIndexName: Bloomberg Global Aggregate Canadian Float Adjusted
headline: The default fixed-income building block for Canadian portfolios.
summary: VAB holds a broad mix of Canadian government and investment-grade corporate bonds across the maturity spectrum. It's the diversified, low-fee 'I just need bonds' answer for Canadian investors.
mechanics: Pays monthly distributions in CAD. Duration around 7-8 years, so sensitive to interest rate moves. The fund holds federal, provincial, and corporate bonds in proportion to the index.
whoItsFor: Investors building a balanced portfolio who want a single, cheap, diversified fixed-income holding. Best held in an RRSP or TFSA for tax efficiency.
